Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Understand the Users Goal The user wants a DuckDB SQL query that filters out cases from an event log table based on two conditions The case contains a specific sequence of three activities in direct succession Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Confirm Order The final result should return all events from cases that do not match these criteria

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Deconstruct the Requirements Input table event_log with columns case_id activity timestamp Condition 1 Direct succession of three specific activities This means for a given case_id if we order events by timestamp there should be three consecutive rows where activity matches the sequence exactly Condition 2 Time difference between the 2nd and 3rd activity in that sequence more than 5 days Condition 3 Exclude cases matching both conditions Return all events from non matching cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Identify Key SQL Concepts Needed Window functions LAG or LEAD to check consecutive activities within a case ordered by timestamp Time difference calculation timestamp diff or date arithmetic in DuckDB Filtering Exclusion NOT IN or EXCEPT or LEFT JOIN IS NULL or NOT EXISTS

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 1 Identify consecutive sequences per case We need to order events per case by timestamp and check if three consecutive rows match the sequence Using LAG and LAG twice if we use LAG for the current row being Confirm Order prev act1 would be Create Order and prev act2 would be Approve Purchase Requisition The timestamp for Create Order would be prev ts1 The timestamp for Confirm Order is the current timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets verify this logic If a case has exactly this sequence the row for Confirm Order will satisfy these conditions What if there are multiple such sequences in one case The requirement says Identifies any case that contains the following sequence So if it happens at least once the case is excluded

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 2 Identify cases to exclude Select distinct case_id from ordered_events where activity Confirm Order and prev activity 1 Create Order and prev activity 2 Approve Purchase Requisition and timestamp minus prev timestamp 1 more than interval 5 days

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: DuckDB timestamp subtraction returns an interval Comparing interval to interval works Alternatively date diff day prev ts1 timestamp more than 5 or timestamp more than prev ts1 plus interval 5 days I will use timestamp minus prev ts1 more than interval 5 days as it is standard and clean in DuckDB

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 3 Filter the original table Select from event_log where case_id not in select case_id from cases_to_exclude order by case_id timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Direct succession The LAG approach correctly checks direct succession because it looks at the immediately preceding rows ordered by timestamp If there is any other event in between the LAG values will not match the required activities Time difference timestamp minus prev timestamp 1 more than interval 5 days handles this correctly Multiple matches per case Select distinct case_id handles it Final output return all events from event_log except those that belong to cases matching both conditions The query does exactly that

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: Timestamp ties What if two events have the exact same timestamp The order might be ambiguous Usually in process mining we assume timestamps are unique per case or we add a secondary sort key like an event id The prompt does not mention an event id so I will order by timestamp

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Self Correction Verification Check LAG offsets LAG activity 1 is the immediate predecessor LAG activity 2 is the one before that So for the current row Confirm Order prev1 is Create Order prev2 is Approve Purchase Requisition This matches the sequence Approve Create Confirm Correct Check timestamp difference The requirement says time elapsed between the Create Order event and the Confirm Order event prev timestamp 1 corresponds to Create Order timestamp corresponds to Confirm Order so timestamp minus prev timestamp 1 is correct

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Potential issue with ties If timestamps are identical ORDER BY timestamp might not be deterministic I will add a note about it or use ORDER BY timestamp activity Since the prompt does not provide an event id I will stick to timestamp

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Alternative approach Could use MATCH RECOGNIZE DuckDB does not natively support MATCH RECOGNIZE yet Window functions are safer

Name: Validation - PE
Text: One minor thing NOT IN with a subquery can sometimes be slower or behave oddly with NULLs but case_id is an identifier so it is fine Potential edge case What if a case has multiple sequences and only one meets the time condition The case is still excluded The prompt says if any instance matches both the whole case is excluded Select distinct case_id handles this
